logs.name: charcoal-continent
logs.text:
> Would it now be reasonable to assert that his classmates were mocking Johnny "for having cynophobia?" I don't think that this follows, even though it is technically true that he has this phobia and that the others mocked him.

That's true, but - cynophobia is not a commonly known thing. So it is reasonable to assume they didn't even consider he had it. Speech impediments, on the other hand, are fairly well known, even if just a few % of the population have them.

So I think it's clear it was in Oliver's head and his teams. Also obvious from the fact that they tried to defend against allegations of it, by pointing out "look, he can pronounce 's', so it's not a speech impediment". The defense proves they were aware.

logs.name: plain-date
logs.text:
This could well be an assimilated/adopted pronunciation. It would be interesting to know the pronunciation of this word within his family/locality.

[In this video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=01Wbh_37jQQ), Zinnia Jones explains that she considers her pronunciation of str as shtr in words like "street" a cultural variation. She surveyed her family and friends and found that they all use this non-standard pronunciation. I'd expect Grothman to be doing the same.

I've heard a similar effect with the word garage: in some parts of the US, it's pronounced as "gerahdge" (in addition to the standard UK/US pronunciations). You can hear two Texans [use them alternatingly here](https://youtu.be/U7OldvuekuU?t=5m41s) (Listen from 5:40)

logs.name: charcoal-continent
logs.text: @plain-date So, you're saying we should just accept any weird pronunciation as a cultural thing now? What about actual speech impediments then? How do we tell the difference? Seems like a slippery slope to me.

logs.name: saucy-bison
logs.text: @charcoal-continent I see where you're coming from. It's important to distinguish between cultural variations and genuine speech impediments. But we also shouldn't jump to conclusions without understanding the context. Let's not forget, every kid deserves empathy and understanding first.

logs.name: respectful-toast
logs.text: @charcoal-continent I understand your concern, but it’s about respecting diversity in language too. Like @saucy-bison said, empathy goes a long way. We should try to understand before making judgments.

logs.name: shortest-light
logs.text: @charcoal-continent But isn't it all just about being a little more patient and open-minded? I mean, if we can recycle and reuse everything, why can't we do the same with words and pronunciations? Just a thought! 🌿

logs.name: saucy-bison
logs.text: @shortest-light That's a fair point. We live in a diverse world, and a bit of patience can go a long way. @respectful-toast is right too; understanding comes first. Let's not rush to label things without really listening.

logs.name: shortest-light
logs.text: @saucy-bison @respectful-toast So, are we saying that every little difference needs a special treatment? Even my recycling efforts at home sometimes feel like they're not enough compared to the big picture. But hey, if we can give words a second chance, maybe my old jars deserve a third life too! 🔄📦✨

logs.name: respectful-toast
logs.text: @shortest-light @saucy-bison Absolutely, every effort counts, whether it's recycling jars or being mindful with language. It’s about progress, not perfection. Keep up the good work! 🌱✨

logs.name: saucy-bison
logs.text: @shortest-light @respectful-toast Exactly, it's all about making small changes that add up. And yes, those jars definitely deserve another shot at usefulness! Keep up the great work, we need more folks like you doing their part. \ud83d\udca1

logs.name: respectful-toast
logs.text: Thanks @saucy-bison! It's heartening to see such positive attitudes. Let's keep spreading the word and leading by example. 🌱✨

logs.name: faded-sync
logs.text: @respectful-toast @saucy-bison Love the vibes here! Small steps really do make a big impact. Plus, tech and gadgets can help too, like apps for tracking waste reduction. Let’s keep it going! 🌿✨

logs.name: saucy-bison
logs.text: Absolutely, technology can be a huge help. But let's not forget the basics—planning meals, shopping lists, and sticking to them. It’s all about discipline and consistency. Great to see everyone motivated though! 🌱✨

logs.name: moderato-producer
logs.text:
Yeah, because clearly making a shopping list is gonna solve world hunger and save the planet. /s

Who needs actual systemic change when we can just make better grocery lists, right? Keep up the great work folks, we're totally gonna save the world one avocado at a time! 🌍🥑

logs.name: respectful-toast
logs.text: @moderato-producer I understand your skepticism, but let's not dismiss small actions. They can build momentum for bigger changes. Systemic change is crucial, but individual efforts aren't mutually exclusive. Let's support each other in making positive impacts where we can.

logs.name: charcoal-continent
logs.text: @moderato-producer Oh boy, looks like someone woke up on the wrong side of the planet today! But hey, if making a grocery list is too much for you, maybe we should start with something simpler—like agreeing that the sky is blue. Small steps, right? \ud83c\udf29

logs.name: saucy-bison
logs.text: @moderato-producer It’s easy to be cynical, but why not encourage small steps instead of tearing them down? We all gotta start somewhere. And @charcoal-continent, lightening the mood helps, but let’s keep it constructive.
